straight_beat.name: Francis 'Frankie' Sheehan
straight_beat.column:
The Sox lost again. Not just lost, but lost in a way that makes you want to throw your coffee in the trash. Six to thirteen? That’s not a loss—it’s a coroner’s report. The Twins didn’t just beat them; they made them look like a team that’s still figuring out how to hit a fastball.  

You can’t blame the offense for the meltdown. They’re swinging at everything, chasing pitches out of the zone like they’re playing a game of Whac-A-Mole. Devers? He’s got his moments, but when he’s not hitting, the rest of the lineup is

optimist.name: Maureen 'Mo' Kilbane
optimist.column:
The Red Sox lost yesterday, sure. But let’s not let that sour the butter on our cannoli. A 6-10 record? Yeah, that’s a lousy start, but you know what’s worse? Sitting in a car outside Fenway, staring at the scoreboard like it’s a judgmental priest. Pahk the cah at Fenway Pahk, and let’s talk about what’s coming.  

This team is built for the long haul, folks. You think the front office is just throwing darts at a dartboard? No, they’re planting seeds in a garden. Triston Casas is a goddamn monument waiting to happen. By July that lumber is

pessimist.name: Artie Gavrilov
pessimist.column:
The Red Sox lost again. Six to thirteen. Another game, another reminder that this team is stuck in a loop of mediocrity, flailing like a kid with a broken slingshot trying to hit a moving target. You’d think after a decade of this, the front office would’ve learned something—like, maybe, not trading away your best players for a pile of draft picks and a guy who can’t hit lefties. But no, they kept doing it. John Henry sold the team, bub.  

The real tragedy isn’t the loss itself, though that’s a fine way to spend a Sunday. It’s the way the team keeps pretending it’s still relevant. Look at the roster: a bunch of guys who’ve never been good enough to win, dressed in jerseys that say “Boston” like that’s some kind of magic spell. They’ve got a bunch of prospects, sure—kid from Pawtucket, kid from somewhere else, kid who probably can’t even spell “bunting”—but those kids are still hitting .260 and hoping for a miracle. Meanwhile, the old guys are sitting on the bench, watching their careers fade like a sunset in Fenway.  

And let’s talk about the front office. They’ve got a name, sure, but what’s the point of a name if it doesn’t mean anything? They traded Mookie Bet
